mnemonics.title: SLoMPH - Types of OS Structures
mnemonics.content: SLoMPH stands for: Simple, Layered, Microkernel, Polymorphic/Hybrid. This covers the main OS structure types in order of evolution.

mnemonics.title: MILD - Monolithic Properties
mnemonics.content: MILD stands for: Multiple procedures, Integrated services, Linked together, Direct function calls. Monolithic OS has all functions in kernel.

mnemonics.title: LION - Layered Approach Benefits
mnemonics.content: LION stands for: Isolation between layers, Organized structure, Nutshell (simple to understand), Easy debugging. Each layer only uses services of layer below.

mnemonics.title: KISS - Microkernel Principle
mnemonics.content: KISS stands for: Keep It Small and Simple. Microkernel provides only essential functions (IPC, basic scheduling, memory management) - everything else runs as user processes.

mnemonics.title: CCS - Client-Server Model
mnemonics.content: CCS stands for: Client requests service, Central server manages resources, Server responds to client. Used in distributed systems and microkernel-based OS.

mnemonics.title: HARM - Hybrid OS Advantages
mnemonics.content: HARM stands for: Hybrid combines best, Advantages of multiple designs, Reuses proven concepts, Maintains compatibility. Windows and macOS are hybrid examples.

acronyms.usage: Originally a pun on Multics. Classic example of monolithic structure with layers. Used to understand OS design philosophy.
Standard API for OS compatibility. Important when studying how OS structures provide consistent interfaces.
Critical for microkernel and client-server structures. Used by processes to communicate across kernel-user boundary.
How applications interact with OS services. Different structures expose different APIs to programmers.
Often implemented as server process in microkernel/client-server. Example: Windows Explorer, macOS Finder.
Often uses microkernel for reliability. Must meet strict timing constraints.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a big pizza with all toppings mixed together in one base - everything is in one layer, all functions (process, memory, file, device management) are tightly coupled and communicate directly. No separation between layers.
Think of a wedding cake or a tall building with floors. Each floor (layer) can only use the floor below it. Bottom floor = hardware, top floor = user interface. You can only go down, not skip floors.

Picture a small core (heart) with separate rooms (servers) connected by hallways (IPC). The core only handles essential tasks - everything else (file systems, drivers, networking) runs in separate protected rooms.
Imagine a restaurant - customers (clients) place orders, the waiter (IPC) delivers to kitchen (server), kitchen prepares food and returns it. Many clients can be served by one server.
Think of a chameleon - it combines features from both monolithic and microkernel. Like Windows NT kernel that has Unix subsystem compatibility plus microkernel-like design.

Imagine a VIP area (kernel mode) with full access to everything, versus a general public area (user mode) with limited access. Programs in user mode must ask permission (system calls) to enter VIP area.

analogies.analogy: Like a small village where everyone knows everyone and can directly talk to anyone. Fast communication but if one person causes trouble (bug), it affects everyone. Early UNIX, Linux kernels work this way.

Like a company's hierarchy - CEO → Manager → Supervisor → Worker. Each level only gives orders to the level below. Easy to fix problems at one level without disturbing others. Like a well-organized bureaucracy.

Like a government with minimal essential services (defense, courts) in central power, while other services (education, transportation) are outsourced to private companies. If one service fails, others continue. Minix and QNX use this.
Like a bank - customers (clients) make requests, bank tellers (servers) process transactions. Multiple customers can be served, and the server manages shared resources (money/account info).

Like building a custom house where you have complete control over every material. The OS only provides basic protection, applications choose their own abstractions. Very flexible but complex to program.

Like choosing between a sports car (fast but complex, like monolithic) vs. a reliable sedan (slower but easier to maintain, like microkernel). Hybrid tries to get benefits of both - like a luxury car with sport features.
